paragraphs.body: Late August and early September of each year in Cedar Grove, New Jersey always felt nostalgic. Driving down Pompton Ave with my best friends blasting Taylor Swift to get a BEC with SPK was a part of the weekly routine. We would sit in the Hot Bagels n More parking lot and watch every person go in and out. Amongst everyone in the car, we knew at least eighty percent of the people who would walk in and out of the store. Since no one would ever want to go home quite yet, we would drive through Little Falls past Rita's, past QuickChek, past PVHS, and all the way around back to CG. Then we would sit in the CGHS or CGTP lot and talk for hours anticipating the upcoming school year. Once September hit, I always knew exactly what to expect. It was time to start preparing for the same routine I had known for eighteen years. I knew every teacher in the district and all one hundred and twenty-two kids in my small grade since we were five years old. The first few days of school were always super exciting, but then after a few weeks it all started to feel like there was never a summer break to begin with. Driving through my little suburban town in the perfect warmish-cool weather at eight in the morning during those first few weeks, I would always think of my replies when it was time for icebreakers. “What are you excited for this school year? Tell a fun fact about yourself. What did you do this summer?” Everyone's replies to “What did you do this summer?” was always without a doubt “I went down the shore a lot.” “Something me, my friends, and basically everyone I know does frequently is go down the shore.” I said this to my roommate, who is from Chicago, the first time we ever spoke on facetime, and she looked confused. “Down the shore?” she said questioningly. I had no idea that other people were unfamiliar with the phrase ‘down the shore’ or the abbreviation ‘dts’ if you are texting someone about it. Going “down the shore” just means going to the beach, which is only about an hour away from my hometown. It was such a culture shock that I couldn’t use that phrase anymore because it was something I said multiple times a day back at home. Another thing that only my New Jersey friends could understand is bagels. Our local bagel shop gains an appearance from my friends and I when we’re low on money, after a night out, or even during lunch all throughout high school. If I ever say I want a bagel when I am here at school, my college friends tell me to go to Einstein Bros or Dunkin Donuts and get a bagel, but it just isn’t a bagel to me. I’ve tried explaining to them how it isn’t the same, but they can’t understand. I want to go to my small, local bagel shop with the worker who knows me and my friend's orders before we even speak. Only my Jersey friends understand what I mean when I say I want a bagel. A vital part of my identity is obviously where I grew up, in New Jersey. When I speak in my New Jersey language I feel confident, strong-minded, and comfortable. I have a more outgoing personality, so I feel like I come across louder and intimidating when I speak my New Jersey language to people who may not speak the same language as me.

annotations.text: “I went down the shore a lot.”
annotations.comment: This moment feels meaningful. Let's see how it plays out!

paragraphs.body: Bagels being a huge part of my New Jersey culture is only half of it. Bagels are a common Jewish food that I frequently enjoy with my family. I grew up in a reformed Jewish household where nothing was ever that serious. I personally am not religious at all, but Jewish slang is still something I grew up around and something I hear each time I go home. Schmatta is a common one frequently said amongst my family, specifically my grandma. A schmatta is just any kind of towel, cloth, or covering. Another frequent one used in my house is the word “kvetch. To “kvetch” just means to complain. For instance, every time I had homework to complain about my mom would tell me to ‘“stop kvetching” and do my work.’ Another common word I hear a lot when I’m at home is “schlep.” To schlep means to drag something around, but in my house, it was used to describe feeling sick. If me or my brother wasn’t feeling good, my mom would ask us “are you sick-sick or just schleppy?” I remember saying the word “kvetch” in front of one of my best friends during my sophomore year of high school and she had no idea what I was saying. I explained it, but she didn’t fully register it which shows this language is secret and personal to my family. My personal favorite word, that I even got my hometown friends to start saying, is “schmutz.” To say there is schmutz on something just means the object is dirty. My grandma uses the word to talk about if someone is swearing or speaking inappropriately. This part of my language makes my loud, New Jersey personality seem a little bit less harsh and way less intimidating because when it comes to family, I am an extremely sensitive and caring person.

annotations.text: grew up in a reformed Jewish household where nothing was ever that serious. I personally am not religious at all, but Jewish slang is still something I grew up around and something I hear each time I go home.
annotations.comment: This is also interesting! It feels like a new and different idea, however, than your Jersey self. Let's talk and find out what the focus of this essay is.

paragraphs.body: I was always taught to never leave an argument with someone you care about on a bad foot. My mom taught me that lesson, but never directly. If we were ever in an argument, my mom would never send me to bed without giving me a “kiss on the keppie.” "Kiss on the keppie" is a saying I heard often growing up. My mom used to say this to me before bed every night when I was younger. She would give me a hug and kiss on my head and then tell me to turn my lights off and go to sleep. I never realized this wasn't a common phrase until my friends asked what that meant one night when they heard my mom say it over facetime and I explained it's just a hug and kiss goodnight. I am fairly certain “keppie” is another Jewish phrase, but I’ve never heard anyone in my family other than my mom use it and therefore it feels more personal and directed from her. Giving or getting a kiss on the keppie is a language I speak with my mom to say “I love you” no matter what the circumstances are in that moment.
